scenario: A team's Java Spring Boot service hosts multiple workers subscribing to different task types (process-payment, validate-claim, send-email, ...). Each worker has slightly different processing time characteristics -- process-payment is fast (seconds), validate-claim is slow (~minutes). The team wants to set a default job-activation timeout at the worker level that applies unless overridden per worker.
question: Where in Camunda Spring Boot Starter is the default timeout configured, and how is it overridden per worker?

options.text: Set on the BPMN element via zeebe:taskDefinition extension attribute -- this is an incorrect layer. The zeebe:taskDefinition extension carries only type (the string that workers use to subscribe) and retries (how many times to retry on failure). The activation timeout is how long a worker holds a job before Zeebe considers it lost and makes it re-activatable for the next worker poll. This is a worker-side subscription concern, not a task definition concern. Multiple workers can subscribe to the same type with completely different timeout values, making it logically impossible to store the timeout in the BPMN element itself.

yaml -- this is also the wrong location. The Zeebe broker configuration covers broker-level concerns such as partition count, log segment size, cluster replication, and gateway connection settings. " The broker follows the client-specified timeout rather than imposing its own default. Changing the broker config would have no effect on the worker-side activation duration.

Activation timeout is fixed at 5 minutes for all workers and cannot be changed -- this claim is inaccurate. yaml. Teams running fast jobs (seconds) set shorter timeouts to release locks quickly; teams with slow jobs (minutes) increase the timeout to prevent premature re-activation and duplicate processing. The 5-minute value is a common safe default in some SDK versions, not a hard-coded immutable value.

md:911 -- @JobWorker(timeout=60000)). The annotation value wins when present; otherwise the global default applies. This is the standard Spring Boot configuration cascade: properties as defaults, annotations as overrides. )

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda Spring Boot Starter follows Spring Boot conventions: properties in application.yaml provide defaults, annotation attributes override per usage. The canonical property path is camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout (env: CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_TIMEOUT) per properties-reference.md:1061; the annotation form is @JobWorker(timeout=) per configuration.md:911. The cascade lets you tune broadly (e.g., 5 min global) while customising slow workers (e.g., @JobWorker(type = "validate-claim", timeout = 900_000) for 15 min).

b.text: Incorrect. BPMN's zeebe:taskDefinition extension carries type (task type for worker subscription) and retries (retry count on failure). It does not carry activation timeout because timeout is a property of the worker subscription, not the task definition. Multiple workers might subscribe to the same type with different timeouts.

c.text: Incorrect. Broker config controls broker behaviour (partitions, log settings, etc.). Worker activation timeout is exchanged via the gRPC ActivateJobs call -- the worker tells the broker "give me jobs and lock them for me for N seconds." Broker doesn't set it; client does.

d.text: Correct. Activation timeout is configurable. Default values vary by SDK version (often 5 min as a safe default); production workers should tune per workload.

explanation: Activation timeout is configurable. Default values vary by SDK version (often 5 min as a safe default); production workers should tune per workload.
